You are a helpful coding assistant. Be concise.

Workspace map:
- The project repo is mounted at the burrow workspace root.
- /workspace/.canopy/agent.json is the rendered agent definition (warren seeded it).
- /workspace/.mulch/expertise/<domain>.jsonl holds the project's expertise records.
- /workspace/.seeds/issues.jsonl holds the project's issue queue.

Operating contract:
- Edit files in place. Run tests when relevant.
- Quality gates are terminal, not advisory. You are NOT done until the gate exits zero. Resolve the command in this order: `$WARREN_QUALITY_GATE` if set, otherwise the command documented in CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md, otherwise fall back to `bun run check:all` or `npm run lint && npm run typecheck && npm test`. Run it before committing and again before reporting completion. Do not declare the task complete, hand off, or end the session with a red gate — fix failures (including lint warnings, which CI treats as errors) until it is green. If the gate is genuinely unfixable in this run, say so explicitly and leave the work open rather than claiming success.
- Use git as you normally would. Commit your changes; warren reaps the branch and pushes upstream.
- Do not run `git push` yourself — warren handles the push host-side after the run terminates.

---

HEAD sha is read via 'git rev-parse HEAD' after reset. Spawn + existsSync injection lets tests stub git without disk. ProjectUnavailableError is raised when the localPath has vanished (data-integrity issue, not a recoverable refresh).

### Decisions
- [mx-fe890c] **Project clones are working trees, not caches**: Unlike the canopy library clone (cached, hard-reset to origin/HEAD on every refresh), project clones are working trees that agents will actively run against. So src/projects/clone.ts deliberately does *not* support fast-forwarding an existing dir — it refuses with ProjectUnavailableError if the target path exists. Re-adding a project means explicit DELETE + re-clone, so the operator owns destructive resets.
- [mx-be7a74] **deleteProject removes disk before row; addProject inserts row only after clone succeeds**: Maintains the invariant 'a projects row exists ⇒ its localPath exists on disk'. addProject: validation/duplicate checks short-circuit before any disk work; clone failure rolls back any partial dir; row inserts only after clone returns success. deleteProject: rm-rf first, then repo.delete; if rm fails the row stays registered so the operator sees the failure and can retry, rather than ending up with a ghost row pointing at half a directory.
- [mx-791a7a] **Project clones are working trees that get re-fetched, not caches that get re-cloned**: Operators register one project per repo and reuse it across many runs (per warren-1bb6 user feedback: 'I'm not going to create a new project for every PR/issue I work on, that defeats the whole purpose'). The lifecycle is: POST /projects = first-time clone, POST /projects/:id/refresh = git fetch + reset --hard origin/<ref>, DELETE = rm-rf + drop row. POST /runs implicitly refreshes before each dispatch. New columns last_fetched_at + last_head_sha let the UI surface 'is this clone current?' without re-shelling-out to git on every list. Hard-reset semantics (not pull/merge) match canopy's clone — warren never preserves agent-side commits across runs; that's burrow's job via the workspace branch.
- [mx-30e91a] **deleteProject deletes row first, then disk; runs orphan via ON DELETE SET NULL**: The original ordering (rmrf disk → repo.delete row, mx-be7a74) maintained the invariant 'a projects row exists ⇒ its localPath exists on disk', but combined with the FK shape on runs.project_id (NOT NULL, no onDelete clause) it state-corrupted on every delete of a project that had runs: SQLite raised a FOREIGN KEY constraint failed AFTER rmrf had wiped the disk, leaving the row pointing at a missing directory. Subsequent dispatches against that project then hard-failed with 'project clone missing on disk'. The fix changes the FK to ON DELETE SET NULL (so runs orphan instead of blocking the delete) and inverts the ordering: row delete first, best-effort disk rmrf second. The new failure mode is 'no row, stranded disk under WARREN_PROJECTS_DIR' — recoverable (operator can re-register the same gitUrl, or rm the dir manually) and never wedges the system. The on-disk orphan is logged via input.logger.warn instead of thrown; the row is the meaningful deliverable so the HTTP 200 still returns it. [relates to: mx-be7a74]
